title: Hard Lump in Dog's Mammary Gland: Causes and Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Found a hard lump in your dog's mammary tissue? Learn about mammary tumors, urgency levels, and why you should schedule a vet exam today.
content_html:
What is it?

Discovering a hard lump in your dog's mammary gland can be a frightening experience. These masses are firm growths located within the breast tissue, often near the nipples. In dogs, these can range from benign (non-cancerous) cysts and fibrous tissue to malignant mammary tumors. For unspayed females or those spayed later in life, the risk of these lumps being neoplastic is significantly higher.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a mammary lump is Medium. While it is generally not a "ER right this second" emergency unless the lump is bleeding or the dog is lethargic, it should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. Approximately 50% of mammary masses in dogs are malignant, and early surgical intervention is the most effective way to ensure a positive outcome.

Why a photo helps triage

When you contact your veterinarian, providing a clear, well-lit photograph of the area is incredibly helpful. A photo allows the medical team to see the exact location, check for skin ulceration or redness, and document the initial size. This visual record helps the vet determine how quickly your dog needs to be squeezed into the schedule and serves as a vital baseline for monitoring any rapid changes.

faqs.question: Can male dogs develop mammary lumps?
faqs.answer: Yes. While it is much less common than in females, male dogs do have mammary tissue and can develop both benign and malignant mammary tumors.

faqs.question: Does a hard lump always mean cancer?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Only a veterinarian can determine if a lump is cancerous through a fine-needle aspirate or a biopsy, as some hard lumps may be benign adenomas or localized infections.

faqs.question: Will spaying my dog now make the lump go away?
faqs.answer: No, spaying will not shrink an existing mammary mass. However, your vet may recommend spaying at the same time the mass is surgically removed to prevent future hormonal influences on the remaining mammary tissue.
